name: Dimensionality of spacetime (3+1)
summary: Why does observed spacetime have exactly three spatial dimensions and one time dimension? Tegmark (1997) showed that any combination other than 3+1 leads to either non-hyperbolic (unpredictable) physics, no stable atoms, or no stable orbits — providing an anthropic argument for 3+1. String theory typically requires 9+1 or 10+1 dimensions and posits that the extras are compactified or warped to invisibility. The 'why 3+1' question is the choice between anthropic selection and a dynamical compactification mechanism.

characteristic_numbers.expected: d > 3 + extra dimensions in string / KK theory
characteristic_numbers.note: String theory natively lives in 9+1 (superstrings) or 10+1 (M-theory); compactification produces 4D effective physics. Pure SM + GR has no reason to pick (3, 1).

principles_stressed.note: Anthropic arguments (Tegmark 1997) suggest 3+1 is selected because other dimensionalities don't admit observers. UV completions (string theory + compactification, ADD, RS, dark dimension) provide dynamical answers but require selecting a compactification.

history: Discussed since Ehrenfest (1917) noted no stable orbits for d > 3. Tegmark (1997), 'On the dimensionality of spacetime', Class. Quantum Grav. 14, L69, arXiv:gr-qc/9702052, gave a comprehensive anthropic analysis covering hyperbolicity, atom stability, and orbit stability. String compactifications (Calabi-Yau, flux, dark dimension) are the primary dynamical alternatives.

references.citation: Tegmark, 'On the dimensionality of spacetime', Class. Quantum Grav. 14, L69 (1997)
